interactions.summary: Jiang says Gay Talese made him idealize journalism as truth-seeking for the common man, then argues journalism broke after 2016 through Trump derangement, intelligence-aligned media, Russiagate, COVID, Ukraine coverage, and the contrast with independent media and Tucker Carlson.
The host asks how journalism can normalize for humanity when controlled by state or oligarchical forces, and whether podcasting enables truth-seeking over political correctness.

Jiang says postwar journalism once created a shared national information space, but now that space is splintering into bubbles and corporations are pushing AI assistants to interpret books and news for people.

speaker_notes.note: Performed host question on journalism and podcasting; eligible as an interview question if used later.

claims.claim: Jiang says old-school journalism, as modeled by Gay Talese, was a working-class outsider practice of truth-seeking and recording history from a critical lens.
Jiang says journalism broke after Trump's 2016 victory when media aligned with national-security figures and abandoned fair reporting in episodes like Russiagate, COVID, and Ukraine.
Jiang says AI assistants are the corporate answer to fractured media: they will interpret books and news for users and mediate individual worldviews.

signature_moments.moment: Journalism's truth mission flips into intelligence-aligned anti-Trump narrative management.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why Jiang migrates from legacy press to independent media as truth surface.

signature_moments.moment: The future reader no longer reads; the assistant reads the world and tells the reader what it means.
signature_moments.source_phrase: These AI assistants will read the book for you and tell you what it means
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It extends the attention-control thesis into media consumption.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The legacy information apparatus that once created national narrative and now seeks AI-mediated control as audiences splinter.
The host's contrast term for media that protects approved positions rather than seeking truth.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang marks 2016 as the break point for journalism and Ukraine coverage as the point by which he had given up on legacy journalism.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript names 'Walter Gateleson,' likely an ASR error; avoid relying on that name without audio review.
